sections.heading: In this Act, unless the context otherwise requires —
sections.title: Definitions for legal professional terms
sections.summary: This section clarifies the meanings of specific terms used in the previous section, including 'advocate and solicitor', 'practising certificate', 'regulated foreign lawyer', 'Official Assignee', 'Official Receiver', 'sub-fund', and 'VCC'. These definitions are provided by relevant Acts to ensure consistency in regulatory interpretation.

requirements.text: "advocate and solicitor", "practising certificate" and "regulated foreign lawyer" have the meanings given by section 2(1) of the Legal Profession Act 1966
"Official Assignee" and "Official Receiver" have the meanings given by section 2(1) of the Insolvency, Restructuring and Dissolution Act 2018
"sub-fund" has the meaning given by section 2(1) of the Variable Capital Companies Act 2018
"VCC" has the meaning given by section 2(1) of the Variable Capital Companies Act 2018

sections.source_quote: 2. For the purpose of paragraph 1 — "advocate and solicitor", "practising certificate" and "regulated foreign lawyer" have the meanings given by section 2(1) of the Legal Profession Act 1966; "Official Assignee" and "Official Receiver" have the meanings given by section 2(1) of the Insolvency, Restructuring and Dissolution Act 2018; "sub-fund" has the meaning given by section 2(1) of the Variable Capital Companies Act 2018; "VCC" has the meaning given by section 2(1) of the Variable Capital Companies Act 2018.

sections.title: Prohibition orders and financial institution compliance
sections.summary: A prohibition order against a person restricts their ability to carry on regulated activities or perform relevant functions. Financial institutions must not employ or enter into arrangements with prohibited persons, or use their services, for regulated activities. Violations incur fines up to $150,000 for individuals and $100,000 for institutions. Financial institutions may claim defense if they took reasonable steps to ensure compliance.
sections.dtia_summary: Prohibition orders restrict third-party data processing by financial institutions. DTIA must assess whether financial institutions comply with prohibitions on using prohibited persons' services for data processing activities.

requirements.text: A person against whom a prohibition order is made must comply with the order
Financial institutions must not employ or enter into arrangements with prohibited persons for regulated activities
Financial institutions must not use prohibited persons' services for regulated activities
Financial institutions must take reasonable steps to ensure compliance with prohibition orders
Prohibited persons must inform representatives of prohibition orders
Representatives must cease activities prohibited by the order

sections.source_quote: A person against whom a prohibition order is made must comply with the prohibition order. Where a prohibition order is made against a person (A), a financial institution must not employ or enter into any arrangement with A, or use A's service, whether directly or indirectly — to carry on any activity or business, or provide any service, the carrying on or provision of which is regulated or authorised by the Authority

sections.heading: A prohibition order, or any variation or revocation of a prohibition order, takes
sections.title: Effective date of prohibition orders
sections.summary: Prohibition orders or their variations/revocations take effect on the date specified by the Authority in the order or notice, as per section 9(1). This section establishes the timing mechanism for when prohibition orders become active.
sections.dtia_summary: Prohibition orders become effective on the specified date, impacting data processing activities by financial institutions. DTIA must consider the effective date when assessing compliance with prohibitions.

requirements.text: A prohibition order or variation/revocation takes effect on the date specified by the Authority in the order or notice

sections.source_quote: A prohibition order, or any variation or revocation of a prohibition order, takes effect on the date specified by the Authority in the order or the notice in section 9(1), as the case may be.

sections.heading: The Authority —
sections.title: Public disclosure of prohibition orders
sections.summary: The Authority must publish prohibition orders and their variations/revocations to ensure public awareness. The Authority may also publish additional information about prohibited persons for public interest or investor protection. This transparency requirement supports market oversight.
sections.dtia_summary: Public disclosure of prohibition orders affects data processing by financial institutions. DTIA must assess whether prohibited persons' data is disclosed to the public, impacting data sharing and transparency.

requirements.text: The Authority must publish prohibition orders and variations/revocations in a manner ensuring adequate publicity
The Authority may publish additional information about prohibited persons for public interest or investor protection

sections.source_quote: The Authority must publish the making of a prohibition order under section 7, and the variation or revocation of a prohibition order under section 9, in such manner as the Authority thinks will secure adequate publicity for the fact that the prohibition order was made, varied or revoked, as the case may be

sections.heading: The Authority — (cont.)
sections.title: Definitions for AML/CFT and Financial Supervision
sections.summary: This section defines key terms used in the Financial Services and Markets Act 2022-1, including 'AML/CFT authority', 'foreign financial institution', 'protected information', and 'supervision'. It establishes the legal framework for cross-border financial supervision by specifying what constitutes a foreign country, financial institutions, and regulatory actions. The definitions apply to financial institutions and their regulators in Singapore.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines critical terms for cross-border financial data transfers under AML/CFT frameworks, including foreign financial institutions and supervision mechanisms that impact data sharing with international regulators.

requirements.text: The Authority may provide assistance to an AML/CFT authority of a foreign country if the Authority is satisfied that all conditions are fulfilled
The AML/CFT authority must provide written undertakings on confidentiality and disclosure of information
The Authority may transmit information to an AML/CFT authority without legal restrictions

sections.source_quote: The Authority may, on the request of an AML/CFT authority of a foreign country, provide the assistance referred to in section 20 to the AML/CFT authority, if the Authority is satisfied that all of the following conditions are fulfilled

sections.heading: The purposes of this Division are —
sections.title: Purpose of Assistance for AML/CFT Authorities
sections.summary: This section outlines the purposes for which the Monetary Authority of Singapore provides assistance to foreign AML/CFT authorities, including enabling supervision of foreign financial institutions, conducting investigations into applicable offences, and facilitating supervisory actions. The purposes apply to financial institutions and regulatory authorities in Singapore and foreign countries.
sections.dtia_summary: Specifies the conditions under which Singapore's financial regulator shares data with foreign AML/CFT authorities for cross-border supervision, impacting data transfer for financial compliance.

requirements.text: The Authority provides information to an AML/CFT authority for supervision of foreign financial institutions
The Authority provides information to a domestic authority for investigations into applicable offences
The Authority provides information to a domestic authority for enforcement actions

sections.source_quote: The purposes of this Division are — to enable the Authority to provide information to an AML/CFT authority in connection with the AML/CFT authority's supervision of foreign financial institutions

sections.heading: The Authority may, on the request of a domestic authority, provide the assistance
sections.title: Assistance to Domestic Authorities
sections.summary: This section outlines the conditions under which the Monetary Authority of Singapore provides assistance to domestic authorities, including the requirement that the assistance must be for investigations, enforcement actions, or supervisory actions. The conditions apply to financial institutions and regulatory authorities in Singapore.
sections.dtia_summary: Specifies the conditions for data sharing with domestic authorities, which are relevant for internal financial data transfers within Singapore.

requirements.text: The Authority may provide assistance to a domestic authority if the request is for investigations or enforcement actions

sections.source_quote: The Authority may, on the request of a domestic authority, provide the assistance referred to in section 22 to the domestic authority, if the Authority is satisfied that all of the following conditions are fulfilled

sections.heading: Despite the provisions of any prescribed written law or any requirement imposed
sections.title: Cross-border transfer of AML/CFT information to authorities
sections.summary: The Authority may transmit financial institution information to AML/CFT authorities or domestic authorities for supervision, enforcement, or investigations. Transfers require conditions set by the Authority, including confidentiality safeguards and written undertakings. Financial institutions must comply with these conditions despite confidentiality obligations.
sections.dtia_summary: Transfers to AML/CFT authorities require confidentiality safeguards and written undertakings to protect data during cross-border transfers. This section imposes specific conditions on data sharing with foreign regulators.

requirements.text: Authority may transmit financial institution information to AML/CFT authorities for supervision actions
Authority may transmit financial institution information to domestic authorities for investigations
Transfers require conditions set by Authority including confidentiality safeguards
AML/CFT authorities must provide written undertaking to protect confidentiality

sections.source_quote: 25. Despite the provisions of any prescribed written law or any requirement imposed under any such written law, any rule of law, any contract or any rule of professional conduct, the Authority or any person authorised by the Authority may, on the Authority's own motion, and subject to the satisfaction of such conditions as the Authority may determine, transmit any information in the possession of the Authority or a copy of the information, to — (a) an AML/CFT authority in connection with any supervision or supervisory action by the AML/CFT authority; or (b) a domestic authority in connection with an investigation, an enforcement action or a supervisory action by the domestic authority.

sections.heading: Despite the provisions of any prescribed written law or any requirement imposed (cont.)
sections.title: Conditions for AML/CFT authority inspections
sections.summary: AML/CFT authorities may inspect financial institutions in Singapore with Authority approval under strict conditions. Inspections require confidentiality safeguards, written undertakings, and specific access to financial records. Financial institutions must provide information but may avoid disruptions to normal operations.
sections.dtia_summary: Inspections require confidentiality safeguards and written undertakings to protect data during cross-border transfers. This section imposes specific conditions on data sharing with foreign regulators.

requirements.text: AML/CFT authority may inspect financial institution books with Authority approval
Inspection must be for consolidated supervision purposes
AML/CFT authority must provide written undertaking to protect confidentiality
AML/CFT authority must comply with conditions imposed by Authority

sections.source_quote: 26.—(1) An AML/CFT authority may, with the prior written approval of the Authority and under conditions of secrecy, conduct an inspection in Singapore of the books of a financial institution in accordance with this section, if all of the following conditions are satisfied: (a) the financial institution is one over which the AML/CFT authority exercises consolidated supervision authority, and the inspection is solely for the purpose of such consolidated supervision; (b) the AML/CFT authority — (i) is prohibited by the laws applicable to it from disclosing information obtained by it in the course of the inspection to any other person, except when compelled to do so by the laws or a court of the country or territory where it is established; or (ii) has given to the Authority such written undertaking to protect the confidentiality of the information obtained as the Authority may require; (c) the AML/CFT authority has given a written undertaking to the Authority to comply with such conditions as the Authority may impose under subsection (3); (d) the AML/CFT authority has provided or is willing to provide similar assistance to the Authority.

sections.heading: In this Part, unless the context otherwise requires —
sections.title: Definitions for dispute resolution schemes
sections.summary: This section defines key terms for dispute resolution schemes, including 'approved dispute resolution scheme', 'operator', and 'terms of reference'. These definitions apply to the approval and operation of dispute resolution schemes for financial services disputes.

requirements.text: Approved dispute resolution scheme means scheme approved by Authority under section 31(1)
Operator means person administering dispute resolution scheme
Terms of reference define scope, application, operations and procedures of dispute resolution scheme

sections.source_quote: 30. In this Part, unless the context otherwise requires — "approved dispute resolution scheme" means a dispute resolution scheme approved by the Authority under section 31(1); "operator", in relation to a dispute resolution scheme approved under section 31(1), means a person who administers a dispute resolution scheme; "terms of reference", in relation to an approved dispute resolution scheme, means the terms which define the scope, application, operations and procedures of the approved dispute resolution scheme.

sections.heading: An operator, or a chief executive officer or director of an operator, who is
sections.title: Appeals to Minister for removal decisions
sections.summary: An operator or chief executive officer/director aggrieved by Authority's removal decision may appeal to the Minister within 30 days. The Minister's decision is final.

requirements.text: Operator or chief executive officer/director may appeal to Minister within 30 days

sections.source_quote: 34. An operator, or a chief executive officer or director of an operator, who is aggrieved by a decision of the Authority under section 33(1) may, within 30 days after receiving the direction, appeal in writing to the Minister, whose decision is final.

sections.heading: It is a condition of an approval for any dispute resolution scheme approved under
sections.title: Constitution amendments for dispute resolution schemes
sections.summary: The operator of an approved dispute resolution scheme that is a company must obtain Authority approval before amending its constitution. This ensures regulatory oversight of organizational changes.

requirements.text: Company operator must obtain Authority approval before amending constitution

sections.source_quote: 35. It is a condition of an approval for any dispute resolution scheme approved under section 31(1) that the operator of the approved dispute resolution scheme that is a company must not amend its constitution without the prior approval of the Authority.

sections.heading: No liability shall lie against any mediator, adjudicator or employee of an operator
sections.title: No liability for dispute resolution staff acting with care and good faith
sections.summary: This section states that mediators, adjudicators, and employees of an approved dispute resolution scheme are not liable for actions taken with reasonable care and good faith during dispute resolution. The liability protection applies to those acting within the scope of their duties under the approved scheme. The section does not specify who the operator is or what constitutes reasonable care and good faith.

requirements.text: No liability shall lie against any mediator, adjudicator or employee of an operator of an approved dispute resolution scheme for doing or omitting to do any act

If the act is done or omitted to be done with reasonable care and in good faith in the course of or in connection with any mediation or adjudication of a dispute under the approved dispute resolution scheme

sections.source_quote: No liability shall lie against any mediator, adjudicator or employee of an operator of an approved dispute resolution scheme, for doing or omitting to do any act, if the act is done or omitted to be done with reasonable care and in good faith in the course of or in connection with any mediation or adjudication of a dispute under the approved dispute resolution scheme.

sections.heading: No liability shall lie against any mediator, adjudicator or employee of an operator (cont.)
No liability shall lie against any mediator, adjudicator or employee of an operator (cont.)
sections.title: Court-ordered asset transfer during financial institution control
sections.summary: During the Authority's or statutory manager's control of a relevant financial institution's business, courts may direct former or current executives, directors, employees, agents, auditors, or trustees to transfer property, books, records, or documents to the Authority or statutory manager within a specified period. Individuals must also provide information required for the Authority's or statutory manager's duties within specified timeframes. Failure to comply or providing false information constitutes an offence punishable by fines up to $125,000 or imprisonment for up to 3 years.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes court-ordered asset transfer obligations during financial institution control transitions, impacting data transfer impact assessments by requiring specific documentation and information sharing from personnel during regulatory takeovers.

requirements.text: Court may direct former or current executives, directors, employees, agents, auditors, or trustees to transfer property, books, records, or documents to Authority or statutory manager within specified period
Individuals must provide information required for Authority or statutory manager's duties within specified timeframe
Failure to comply with information requirements constitutes an offence punishable by fines up to $125,000 or imprisonment for up to 3 years

sections.source_quote: During the period when the Authority or statutory manager is in control of the relevant business of a relevant financial institution — (a) the Court may, on an application by the Authority or statutory manager, direct any person who has ceased to be or who is still any chief executive, director, member, executive officer, employee, agent, banker, auditor or office-holder of, or trustee for, the relevant financial institution to pay, deliver, convey, surrender or transfer to the Authority or statutory manager, within such period as the Court may specify, any property, book, accounts, record or other documents, whether in electronic, print or other form, of the relevant financial institution which is comprised in, forms part of or relates to the relevant business of the relevant financial institution, and which is in the person's possession or control; and (b) any person who has ceased to be or who is still any chief executive, director, member, executive officer, employee, agent, banker, auditor or office-holder of, or trustee for, the relevant financial institution must give to the Authority or statutory manager such information as the Authority or statutory manager may require for the discharge of the Authority's or statutory manager's duties or functions, or the exercise of the Authority's or statutory manager's powers, in relation to the relevant financial institution, within such time and in such manner as may be specified by the Authority or statutory manager.

sections.title: Remuneration and expenses for statutory managers
sections.summary: The Monetary Authority of Singapore may determine remuneration and expenses for statutory managers or statutory advisers appointed to relevant financial institutions, whether the appointment has been revoked. The Authority may also set remuneration for itself and any persons appointed under section 179 when it has assumed control of a financial institution's business, regardless of whether control has ceased.
sections.dtia_summary: This section specifies remuneration obligations for statutory managers during financial institution transitions, which may impact data transfer costs and compliance expenses in cross-border financial operations.

requirements.text: Authority may fix remuneration and expenses for statutory managers or statutory advisers appointed to relevant financial institutions, whether appointments have been revoked
Authority may fix remuneration and expenses for itself and persons appointed under section 179 when it has assumed control of relevant financial institution's business

sections.source_quote: The Authority may at any time fix the remuneration and expenses to be paid by a relevant financial institution — (a) to a statutory manager or statutory adviser appointed in relation to the relevant financial institution, whether or not the appointment has been revoked; and (b) where the Authority has assumed control of the relevant business of the relevant financial institution, to the Authority and any person appointed by the Authority under section 179 in relation to the Authority's assumption of control of the relevant business, whether or not the Authority has ceased to be in control of the relevant business.

sections.heading: The Authority may at any time fix the remuneration and expenses to be paid by a (cont.)
sections.title: Director and executive officer removal requirements for financial institutions
sections.summary: Relevant financial institutions must obtain prior written consent from the Authority before permitting a person to act as an executive officer or director if the person has been convicted of fraud or dishonesty, is an undischarged bankrupt, has an enforcement order against them, has entered into a compromise with creditors, has a prohibition order, or has been a director of a wound-up or revoked regulated financial institution. The Authority may also remove directors or executive officers who have wilfully contravened the Act, failed to secure compliance, or failed to discharge duties, following notice and a show-cause period.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes conditions for removing directors/executive officers from financial institutions, which impacts DTIA by requiring assessments of personnel eligibility and governance structures to ensure compliance with regulatory consent and removal procedures.

requirements.text: Relevant financial institution must not permit a person to act as its executive officer without prior written consent of the Authority
Relevant financial institution must not permit a person to act as its director without prior written consent of the Authority
Person must not have been convicted of fraud or dishonesty in Singapore or elsewhere
Person must not be an undischarged bankrupt in Singapore or elsewhere
Person must not have an enforcement order against them for a judgment debt returned unsatisfied
Person must not have entered into a compromise or scheme of arrangement with creditors that is still in operation
Person must not have a prohibition order
Person must not have been a director of a wound-up or revoked regulated financial institution
Authority may direct removal of director or executive officer who has wilfully contravened the Act
Authority may direct removal of director or executive officer who has failed to secure compliance with the Act
Authority may direct removal of director or executive officer who has failed to discharge duties
Authority must give written notice to financial institution and person before directing removal
Authority must allow financial institution and person to show cause why they should not be removed

sections.source_quote: a relevant financial institution must not, without the prior written consent of the Authority, permit a person to act as its executive officer; and a relevant financial institution which is established or incorporated in Singapore must not, without the prior written consent of the Authority, permit a person to act as its director

sections.title: Financial institution compromise and arrangement requirements
sections.summary: The Authority has specific powers over financial institutions during insolvency proceedings, including the right to vote in certain contexts, the requirement for consent before approving compromises or arrangements involving transfers of assets, and the authority to require financial institutions to prepare recovery plans. This section applies to different types of financial institutions with varying requirements for compromise arrangements.
sections.dtia_summary: This section impacts DTIA by requiring assessments of financial institution insolvency procedures and recovery plans to ensure compliance with regulatory consent for asset transfers during restructuring.

requirements.text: Authority has same powers and rights as a creditor in insolvency proceedings
Authority does not have the right to vote at meetings of financial institutions
Court must not approve compromise arrangements for Type A financial institutions without Minister's consent
Court must not approve transfers of assets for Type B financial institutions without Authority's consent
Court must not approve transfers of assets for Type C financial institutions without Authority's consent

sections.source_quote: In any proceedings under section 210 of the Companies Act 1967 or section 71 of the Insolvency, Restructuring and Dissolution Act 2: The Authority has the same powers and rights as a creditor of the company under the Companies Act 1967 or the Insolvency, Restructuring and Dissolution Act 2018

sections.heading: Regulations made under section 192 for this Part may prescribe —
sections.title: Regulatory offences and penalties for financial institutions
sections.summary: Regulations made under section 192 may prescribe offences for contraventions of the regulations, with fines up to $250,000 for individuals and $250,000 for financial institutions, and penalties for continuing offences. The regulations may also specify different persons and classes of persons for different financial institutions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section impacts DTIA by establishing penalties for non-compliance with regulatory requirements, which affects risk assessments for financial institutions.

requirements.text: Contravention of regulations may be punishable by a fine not exceeding $250,000
Continuing offences may be punishable by a further fine not exceeding $25,000 per day

sections.source_quote: any contravention of a provision of the regulations shall be an offence punishable (despite section 192(2)(d)) — (i) in the case of an individual, with a fine not exceeding $125,000 or with imprisonment for a term not exceeding 3 years or with both and, in the case of a continuing offence, with a further fine not exceeding $12,500 for every day or part of a day during which the offence continues after conviction

sections.title: Definitions of key terms for financial institutions
sections.summary: This section defines critical terms used throughout the Financial Services and Markets Act 2022-1, including 'affected person', 'business', 'co-operative society', 'director', 'excluded financial institution', 'executive officer', 'office-holder', 'pertinent financial institution', 'PPF Agency', 'PPF Funds', 'Registrar of Companies', 'Registrar of Co-operative Societies', 'relevant Act', 'relevant provisions', 'significant business', 'specified financial institution', and 'Take-over Code'. These definitions establish the scope and meaning of terms for regulatory purposes in Singapore's financial sector.

sections.heading: In determining whether to exercise its powers under Divisions 2, 4, 5 and 6 of this
sections.title: Criteria for exercising powers over financial institutions
sections.summary: This section outlines the criteria the Monetary Authority of Singapore (MAS) may consider when determining whether to exercise powers under Divisions 2, 4, 5, and 6 of this Part in relation to a pertinent financial institution. It specifies that the Authority may take into account whether a failure of the institution would have widespread adverse effects on Singapore's financial system or economy, whether it is in the public interest, and any other matters the Authority deems relevant.

requirements.text: The Authority may have regard to whether a failure of the pertinent financial institution would have a widespread adverse effect on the financial system in Singapore or the economy of Singapore, or both
The Authority may have regard to whether it is in the public interest to exercise powers
The Authority may have regard to any other matter that the Authority considers relevant

sections.source_quote: 59. In determining whether to exercise its powers under Divisions 2, 4, 5 and 6 of this Part in relation to a pertinent financial institution, the Authority may have regard to one or more of the following matters: (a) whether a failure of the pertinent financial institution would have a widespread adverse effect on the financial system in Singapore or the economy of Singapore, or both, whether or not that widespread adverse effect occurs directly or indirectly as a result of the impact of the failure on the financial system in Singapore, on the financial markets in Singapore or on other financial institutions in Singapore; (b) whether it is in the public interest to do so; (c) any other matter that the Authority considers relevant.

sections.heading: In determining whether to exercise its powers under Divisions 2, 4, 5 and 6 of this (cont.)

sections.title: Definitions for business transfers in financial services
sections.summary: This section defines key terms used in business transfers between financial institutions, including 'business', 'transferor', 'transferee', 'specified business', and 'certificate of transfer'. It establishes the legal framework for transferring parts of a financial institution's operations across Singapore, requiring the transferor to be a pertinent financial institution and the transferee to be a regulated entity. The definitions specify that 'business' includes affairs, property, rights, obligations, and liabilities, while 'specified business' refers to parts of the transferor's business identified in a certificate.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines critical terms for business transfers, ensuring clarity on what constitutes a transferable business unit and the parties involved. It sets the foundation for cross-border data transfers by specifying that transfers must involve regulated entities and follow defined procedures.

requirements.text: The Authority may make a determination that the whole or any part of the business of a transferor must be transferred to a transferee if the board of directors of the transferee has consented to the transfer
The Authority must be satisfied that the transfer is appropriate, having regard to the interests of depositors, policy owners, or affected persons as applicable
The transfer involves the whole or part of the significant business of the transferor
The transferee must be approved, authorized, designated, recognized, registered, licensed, or otherwise regulated by the Authority to carry on the significant business of the transferor

sections.source_quote: In this Division, unless the context otherwise requires — "business" includes affairs, property, right, obligation and liability; "certificate" means a certificate of transfer issued by the Minister under section 67(1); "debenture" has the meaning given by section 4(1) of the Companies Act 1967; "determination" means a determination made by the Authority under section 66(1); "guaranteed policy moneys" has the meaning given by section 2 of the Insurance Act 1966; "property" includes property, right and power of every description; "specified business" means any part of the business of a transferor which is specified or identified in a certificate; "transferee" means any person to which the whole or any part of a transferor's business is, is to be, or is proposed to be transferred under this Division; "transferor" means a pertinent financial institution the whole or any part of the business of which is, is to be, or is proposed to be transferred under this Division.

sections.title: Definitions for business transfers in financial institutions
sections.summary: This section defines key terms used in the context of business transfers between financial institutions, including 'transferor', 'transferee', 'certificate of transfer', 'onward transfer', and 'reverse transfer'. These definitions establish the framework for understanding how business transfers occur under the Act, particularly when financial institutions transfer their operations or assets to other entities.
sections.dtia_summary: These definitions clarify the terminology for business transfers, which is critical for DTIAs to understand the scope of transfers and the obligations that apply when financial institutions transfer their business operations.

requirements.text: 2nd transferee means the person to which the whole or part of a transferor's business that was transferred to a transferee by a certificate of transfer is or is to be transferred from the transferee in accordance with an onward transfer certificate under section 73
business includes affairs, property, right, obligation and liability
certificate of transfer means a certificate of transfer issued under section 67

transferor means a pertinent financial institution the whole or part of the business of which has been transferred by a certificate of transfer

sections.title: Minister's approval process for share transfers in financial institutions
sections.summary: The Minister must approve determinations made by the Authority regarding share transfers in Singaporean financial institutions. The Minister publishes a notice in the Gazette and provides written notice to the transferor before approving the determination. The Minister considers written representations from the transferor and may approve the determination without modification, subject to modifications agreed by the transferee, or refuse approval. The Minister must ensure the transfer does not compromise financial system stability.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates formal approval processes for share transfers involving financial institutions, requiring public notice and written representations to ensure stability and compliance before cross-border transfers occur.

requirements.text: The Minister must publish a notice in the Gazette and newspapers before approving the determination
The Minister must provide written notice to the transferor specifying the date for written representations
The Minister must consider written representations from the transferor when deciding on approval
The Minister may approve the determination without modification
The Minister may approve the determination subject to modifications agreed by the transferee
The Minister may refuse to approve the determination

sections.source_quote: The Minister must publish in the Gazette and in such newspaper or newspapers as the Minister may determine a notice of the Minister's intention to approve the determination, specifying such particulars as the Minister considers appropriate and the date by which the transferor may make written representations to the Minister.

sections.heading: In this Division — (cont.)
sections.title: Restrictive measures for significant shareholders in financial institutions
sections.summary: This section imposes temporary restrictions on significant shareholders of Singaporean financial institutions following share transfers. These restrictions include prohibitions on enforcement actions, sales, voting rights, and financial transactions related to the affected shares for a specified period to ensure financial system stability. The restrictions apply to both the financial institution and its shareholders.
sections.dtia_summary: Significant shareholders face temporary restrictions on financial activities to maintain system stability after share transfers, relevant for DTIA when assessing cross-border share transfers.

requirements.text: No enforcement orders may be commenced against the share
No steps may be taken to enforce security over the share
Any sale, transfer, or assignment of the share is void
No voting rights may be exercised unless the Minister permits
No payments may be made by the financial institution for the share unless authorized
No winding up proceedings may be initiated for the financial institution

sections.source_quote: No enforcement order or other legal process may be commenced or continued against the share.

sections.title: Definitions for share capital restructuring in financial institutions
sections.summary: This section defines key terms used in the share capital restructuring process, including 'business', 'certificate', 'determination', 'property', 'significant shareholder', 'significant shareholder provisions', and 'subscriber'. These definitions clarify the scope and application of the restructuring provisions for financial institutions in Singapore.
sections.dtia_summary: These definitions establish the terminology for share capital restructuring, which is relevant for DTIA when assessing cross-border share transfers and restructuring activities.

requirements.text: Business includes affairs, property, right, obligation and liability
Certificate means a certificate of restructuring of share capital issued by the Minister
Determination means a determination made by the Authority under section 78(1) or (2)
Property includes property, right and power of every description

sections.title: Conditions for determining foreign resolution recognition
sections.summary: This section details the conditions under which the Monetary Authority of Singapore (MAS) must determine whether to recognize a foreign resolution. It requires that recognition would not have widespread adverse effects on Singapore's financial system, would not result in inequitable treatment of creditors or shareholders, would not be contrary to national or public interest, would not have material fiscal implications for Singapore, and would comply with any prescribed conditions. These conditions ensure that cross-border financial resolutions are handled responsibly.
sections.dtia_summary: Sets conditions for recognizing foreign financial resolutions, ensuring they do not negatively impact Singapore's financial system or create inequitable treatment of stakeholders.

requirements.text: The Authority must make a determination that the foreign resolution should be recognised in whole or in part or should not be recognised

The Authority may make a determination that the foreign resolution should be recognised in whole or in part if it is satisfied that all of the following conditions are fulfilled: (a) recognition of the foreign resolution or part would not have a widespread adverse effect on the financial system in Singapore or the economy of Singapore, whether or not that effect occurs directly or indirectly as a result of the effects of recognising the resolution or part

(b) recognition of the foreign resolution or part would not result in inequitable treatment of any Singapore creditor relative to any other creditor of the foreign financial institution with similar rights, or of any Singapore shareholder relative to any shareholder of the foreign financial institution
(c) recognition of the foreign resolution or part would not be contrary to the national interest or public interest
(d) recognition of the foreign resolution or part would not have material fiscal implications for Singapore
(e) any other condition that is prescribed by regulations made under section 135 for the purposes of this paragraph

sections.source_quote: 103.—(1) This section applies where a foreign resolution authority of a foreign country or territory makes a request to the Authority to recognise a foreign resolution in relation to a foreign financial institution by the foreign resolution authority. (2) The Authority must make a determination that — (a) the foreign resolution should be recognised in whole or in part; or (b) the foreign resolution should not be recognised. (3) The Authority may make a determination that the foreign resolution should be recognised in whole or in part if it is satisfied that all of the following conditions are fulfilled: (a) recognition of the foreign resolution or part would not have a widespread adverse effect on the financial system in Singapore or the economy of Singapore, whether or not that effect occurs directly or indirectly as a result of the effects of recognising the resolution or part; (b) recognition of the foreign resolution or part would not result in inequitable treatment of any Singapore creditor relative to any other creditor of the foreign financial institution with similar rights, or of any Singapore shareholder relative to any shareholder of the foreign financial institution; (c) recognition of the foreign resolution or part would not be contrary to the national interest or public interest; (d) recognition of the foreign resolution or part would not have material fiscal implications for Singapore; (e) any other condition that is prescribed by regulations made under section 135 for the purposes of this paragraph.

sections.summary: This section establishes that individuals and partnerships must hold a valid license from MAS to provide digital token services outside Singapore from a Singaporean place of business. A person is presumed to carry on a secondary business of providing that type of digital token service from a place of business in Singapore regardless whether the provision of that type of digital token service is related or incidental to the primary business

sections.summary: A licensee aggrieved by the Authority's decision under section 149(3)(b) may appeal to the Minister within 30 days. The appeal process applies to decisions on licensees and their officers. The Minister's decision is final. This section establishes a formal review mechanism for licensees who disagree with regulatory decisions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides a mechanism for licensees to appeal regulatory decisions to the Minister, which may impact data transfer assessments if the Authority's decisions affect cross-border data handling.

sections.title: Auditor powers and obligations for licensees
sections.summary: The Authority may appoint auditors for licensees who fail to appoint one themselves. Auditors must conduct annual audits of digital token services, submit reports to the Authority, and report serious breaches or irregularities. Auditors face penalties for non-compliance with reporting duties and must maintain confidentiality of information obtained during audits.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes auditor responsibilities for digital token services, which may affect data transfer assessments if audits involve cross-border data handling or require specific data protection measures.

sections.title: Penalties for offences under this Part without specified penalties
sections.summary: This section establishes penalties for individuals and entities convicted of offences under the Part where no specific penalty is outlined in the legislation. Individuals face fines up to $50,000, while other entities face fines up to $100,000. The section also outlines court powers to issue restraining orders for persistent contraventions and related enforcement mechanisms.

sections.title: Service of documents: permitted methods and electronic alternatives
sections.summary: This section outlines permissible methods for serving documents under the Act. It specifies personal delivery, postal service, fax, email, and electronic service as valid methods for serving documents on individuals, partnerships, and corporations. It also establishes timelines for service effectiveness and requires prior written consent for email service. The section explicitly excludes court proceedings.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines service methods for documents, which may impact how data transfers are executed electronically. Email service requires consent, and electronic service mechanisms could affect cross-border data handling if the recipient is outside Singapore.

sections.title: Exemptions from Financial Services Act requirements
sections.summary: This section allows the Authority to exempt individuals from the Financial Services and Markets Act 2022 or its directions, subject to specified conditions. It also permits the Authority to revoke exemptions or modify conditions. Violations of these conditions result in fines up to $100,000 for organizations and $50,000 for individuals. The term 'licensee' is defined in section 136(1).
